first: New flat. New city. The canal is outside.

neutral: Diesel and water. Limehouse's perfume.
Basin still. Boats still. I'm still.
Narrow Street is narrow. The name is honest.
A heron on the lock gate. Absolutely still.
The church is too big for this street.
Iron and water. Limehouse's two materials.
That warehouse. Flats now. The iron hooks remain.
The canal doesn't end. It turns corners.
Cobblestones underfoot. Older than anything.
Someone was here before. Someone will be after.
The air tastes different near the water.
A window lit. A life in progress.
Limehouse holds its breath at corners.
The walls know more than the maps.

time.morning: Coffee steam from the shop. A signal.
time.afternoon: Afternoon. The hard edges go soft.
time.evening: The canal at dusk. Everything gold and grey.
time.night: Limehouse at night is a different proposition.

rain: Rain on the canal. A million tiny impacts.
The basin vanishes in the downpour.

musician: A hum under the bridge. Low. Constant.
The chain clinks in rhythm. Almost musical.
Something in the water. A frequency. Low.
The whole neighbourhood vibrates. One note.
Every surface here is an instrument.
The city plays itself. Nobody conducts.
Footsteps on stone. A rhythm you didn't choose.
The city hums between its words.
Every door is a different key.
Silence here isn't empty. It's tuned.
photographer: Light through the gap. Perfect exposure.
That shadow has been there for centuries.
The canal reflects a version without people.
That gap between buildings. A perfect frame.
Morning fog softens every hard edge.
A window catches the last light.
The alley narrows into pure contrast.
Reflections in puddles. A second Limehouse.
Brick turns gold at this hour.
The basin holds the entire sky.
wanderer: Cobblestones remember different feet.
The air changes between these walls.
Warmer here. The bricks hold heat.
My feet know this route already.
The wind funnels down Narrow Street.
Damp rises through the soles. Old damp.
The ground slopes toward the water. Always.
A cold pocket near the lock gate.
The city presses close then opens.
Stone underfoot. Then gravel. Then stone.
barista: Two strangers. Same bench. Almost talking.
He carries her coat. She doesn't notice.
She nodded at him. He missed it.
The regulars orbit the same places.
A couple arguing. Quietly. About nothing.
Someone is always waiting for someone.
That man walks here every morning.
Laughter from the boat. Three voices.
The woman at the lock. Always alone.
Same table. Same warmth. They've left a mark.
shopkeeper: That doorframe is older than the building.
Someone polished this railing. Every day.
The sign is new. The shop is not.
Iron fittings. Georgian. Worth knowing.
This wall has been three different things.
A brass hinge. Still working. Still here.
The warehouse remembers what it stored.
Paint over paint over paint over wood.
That lock mechanism. Victorian at least.
Every surface here has been sold twice.

neutral: The arches. Brick curves. Someone's hammering inside.
Market setting up. Dawn. Torch lights.
Bermondsey Street. Old side. New side.
The warehouse. Even daylight feels like night.
Train overhead. Ground vibrates. Then stops.
The gallery. White walls. Absolute quiet.
An arch entrance. Dark inside. Something in there.
The church at the road's end. Oldest thing.
Vinyl in the window. Someone's turntable audible.
The river is close. You can smell it.
The co-working glass. Laptops glowing. Nobody speaks.
Long Lane runs straight. Too straight, somehow.
The Thames path. The water keeping pace.
Bermondsey Street ends at the church. Always has.

time.morning: Bermondsey wakes busy. No time for stretching.
time.afternoon: Afternoon. The galleries open. The arches open.
time.evening: The galleries close. The restaurants open. The shift.
time.night: The warehouse guard starts his round. A torch.

rain: Rain on the railway arches. Percussion.
The warehouse vanishes in fog. The guard's torch.

musician: The arch amplifies everything. A private concert hall.
A vinyl record. Forty years old. Still alive.
The overground rattle makes the arch walls resonate.
Brick and iron. Bermondsey's two pitches.
Under the arches: a sound like breathing.
The vinyl shop spins something. Bass through brick.
Market trolleys at dawn. A scattered percussion.
The church bell. One note over the warehouses.
The Thames path. The river's low constant drone.
The co-working hum. Machines pretending to be silent.
photographer: Gallery white. The art competes with nothing.
The rooftop. From here the streets spell something.
The market at dawn. Long shadows. No tourists.
The warehouse. Industrial angles. Serious light.
Bermondsey Street. Photographed a thousand times. Still.
The arch mouth. Light dies three steps in.
Vinyl sleeves in the window. Faded square frames.
The Thames path at dusk. River as mirror.
The church spire. A vertical against flat brick.
Market stalls at dawn. Steam catching low light.
wanderer: The church. Oldest building here. It knows.
The Thames path. The bench facing the water.
The warehouse pulls at night. Not yet inside.
The arch. The temperature drops inside.
Long Lane. Nothing remarkable. That's what's wrong.
The Thames path. Wind off the water bites.
Cobbles by the warehouse. The ground tilts down.
The vinyl shop floor. Worn soft by browsing.
The market at dawn. Cold rises through soles.
The church step. Stone hollowed by centuries.
barista: The co-working space. Everyone stranger, everyone connected.
Market vendors nod to each other. A language.
The gallery. The same visitors, different Thursdays.
The vinyl shop. The owner knows every buyer.
The street corner. Something always almost happens.
The co-working desk. Strangers sharing an outlet.
Two browsers reach for the same record.
The arch workshop. The welder waves. Almost.
The Thames path bench. Always someone just leaving.
The church doorway. People shelter, then part.
shopkeeper: The market object. Wrong materials. Wrong era.
These arches predate the railway. Someone else built.
The church crypt. Something in the floor.
Bermondsey was a market town. The street remembers.
The warehouse floor. A depth marker. Checked yearly.
The vinyl. Pressed before my birth. Still spinning.
Arch brickwork. Hand-laid. Soot-black. Permanent.
The church font. Stone that outlived its parish.
Warehouse iron rails. Built to carry, still carrying.
The market's oldest stall. The same scales. Brass.

neutral: The park smells like cut grass and history.
Tourists at the Cutty Sark. They don't see.
Clocks everywhere. All disagreeing slightly.
The bench is empty. Not yet dusk.
The meridian line. Everyone stands on it. Grins.
The river is wide here. Older.
Stone and symmetry. The city planned this part.
Observatory above. The hill knows what it holds.
Old wood and river light. The pub waits.
The tunnel. Cream tiles. The city crossing itself.
The foreshore at low tide. The river retreating.
Time starts here. The brass line says so.
Masts of the Cutty Sark. Dry-docked. Still.
The hill watches the whole city age.

time.morning: Greenwich wakes slowly. Like it's thinking.
time.afternoon: Afternoon light on the white stone. Sharp.
time.evening: The observatory closes. The sky stays open.
time.night: The park at night. Different country.

rain: The cobbles shine. Everything doubled in reflections.
Rain on the meridian. The brass runs wet.

musician: A hum under the clockmaker's floor. Low.
The tunnel has a note. E-flat. Sustained.
Forty clocks. Forty different pitches.
The park at dawn. Wind in the chestnuts.
The river carries sound differently here.
The foot tunnel hums one held chord.
The Cutty Sark's rigging sings in wind.
The meridian. A frequency you can almost hear.
Foghorns on the river. The lowest note.
The observatory dome. Sound spirals up inside.
photographer: The Naval College. Symmetry as composition.
The foreshore at low tide. Everything exposed.
River light through the pub's unwashed glass.
The hill frames the city. A viewfinder.
The tunnel's perspective. Vanishing point below.
The observatory at dusk. Dome catching last light.
The meridian brass. A line splitting the frame.
Cutty Sark masts. Black against pale sky.
The park's avenue. Trees framing the distance.
Low tide mudflats. Grey on grey on silver.
wanderer: The hill rises and the city drops behind.
The tunnel's midpoint. Something resists the step.
The foreshore clay. Older than the path.
The bench. Worn to the shape of waiting.
The park before dawn. The oldest feeling.
Climbing to the observatory. The legs remember.
The foreshore pulls. Down toward the water.
Cold tiles underfoot. The tunnel breathes damp.
The meridian line. One foot each side.
The foreshore shingle shifts. The river took it.
barista: Coffee from three directions in the market.
The pub landlord knows everyone's name.
She's working there again. Same corner table.
Market vendors nodding to each other. A language.
The hymnal page. Someone's handwriting. Urgent.
Tourists photograph each other on the meridian. Smiling.
The clockmaker nods. He almost remembers me.
Two strangers share the foreshore. Heads down.
The tunnel. We pass without a word.
The naval pensioner feeds gulls. Same bench.
shopkeeper: The clockmaker's hands. Always adjusting something.
That floor stone. Older than the building.
The cobblestones remember ships. Dockyard memory.
Brass and oak. Nothing here is decorative.
The market vendor handles objects too carefully.
The meridian brass. A million soles wore it.
The Cutty Sark's hull. Copper older than memory.
The naval college gates. Iron that outlasts crowns.
Foreshore finds. Clay pipes. Centuries underfoot.
The observatory's brass instruments. Built to outlive us.

leaving: Door. Street. London. Here we go.
The notebook has more pages filled now.
Something to do today. Someone to see.
Lock the door. The city is waiting.
One more walk. One more look.
returning: Home. Or what I'm calling home.
The flat looks different every time.
The radiator. The window. Yours now.
Back. The notebook is where you left it.
